description: In the Evelyn Stealer campaign, threat actors create and distribute a trojanized Visual Studio Code extension that masquerades as a legitimate or useful tool. The extension is uploaded to the Visual Studio Code marketplace, leveraging developer trust in the ecosystem. When a developer installs the extension, it covertly delivers the first stage of the malware, embedding a fake Lightshot.dll component. This approach targets developers specifically, aiming to compromise workstations that often hold sensitive credentials, source code, and cloud access tokens. The use of a familiar extension as the initial infection vector enables the attackers to bypass traditional security controls and blend into normal developer activities.
name: Malicious Visual Studio Code Extension for Initial Access

description: The attackers exploit the Lightshot screenshot tool by dropping a malicious Lightshot.dll into the system. When the legitimate Lightshot.exe is executed—typically during routine screenshot operations—it loads the malicious DLL instead of the genuine one. This side-loading technique allows the malware to execute under the guise of a trusted application, evading detection and blending with normal user behavior. The malicious DLL acts as a loader for subsequent stages of the attack, initiating the download and execution of further payloads while maintaining stealth.
name: DLL Side-Loading via Legitimate Lightshot.exe

description: After the malicious DLL is loaded, the attack chain continues with the execution of hidden PowerShell commands. These commands are used to download a second-stage payload named iknowyou.model from a remote domain, save it as runtime.exe, and execute it. This technique enables the attackers to dynamically fetch updated or additional malware components, increasing flexibility and adaptability. The use of PowerShell for downloading and running executables is stealthy and leverages native system capabilities, making detection more challenging.
name: Staged Payload Download and Execution via PowerShell

description: The Evelyn Stealer payload injects a malicious DLL (abe_decrypt.dll) into browser processes such as Microsoft Edge and Google Chrome. This injection allows the malware to access and extract sensitive information directly from the browsers, including passwords, cookies, and session data. By operating within the context of trusted browser processes, the attackers can bypass some security controls and harvest credentials used for cloud services, messaging platforms, and cryptocurrency wallets.
name: Browser Process Injection for Credential Theft

description: Once deployed, Evelyn Stealer systematically collects a wide range of sensitive data from the compromised developer workstation. This includes browser passwords, cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, messaging sessions, VPN profiles, Wi-Fi keys, and files containing source code or credentials. The malware also captures screenshots and gathers detailed system information. All collected data is compressed into a single archive, preparing it for exfiltration. The breadth of data targeted reflects the attackers' focus on developer environments, aiming to maximize the impact and potential for lateral movement within organizations.
name: Harvesting and Collection of Sensitive Data

description: After aggregating the stolen data into a compressed archive, the Evelyn Stealer malware uploads the package to an attacker-controlled FTP server. This exfiltration method uses standard FTP protocols to transfer the data, which may evade some network monitoring solutions if FTP traffic is allowed or not closely scrutinized. The use of FTP for data exfiltration is straightforward but effective, enabling the attackers to quickly and reliably retrieve large volumes of sensitive information from compromised developer machines.
name: Data Exfiltration via FTP Upload

description: A custom technique in this campaign involves weaponizing the inherent trust developers place in their toolchain and the Visual Studio Code marketplace. By carefully crafting extensions that appear legitimate and useful, attackers exploit the tendency of developers to install and use marketplace tools without rigorous scrutiny. This social engineering approach is tailored to the developer environment, increasing the likelihood of successful compromise and subsequent access to high-value assets such as source code and cloud credentials.
name: Abuse of Developer Toolchain Trust

description: The Evelyn Stealer payload establishes persistence by creating a dedicated folder within the AppData directory named 'Evelyn.' This folder is used to store malicious components and facilitate ongoing operations, such as browser injection and data collection. By leveraging the AppData location, the malware avoids detection by users and some security tools, ensuring it remains active across system reboots and user sessions.
name: Persistence via AppData Folder Creation

description: Evelyn Stealer is an information-stealing malware delivered via a trojanized Visual Studio Code extension. It executes a multistage attack chain, harvesting credentials, browser data, cryptocurrency wallets, messaging sessions, VPN profiles, Wi-Fi keys, and sensitive files, then exfiltrates them to an attacker-controlled FTP server.
name: Evelyn Stealer

description: The operators behind the Evelyn Stealer campaign use malicious Visual Studio Code extensions to deploy multistage information-stealing malware targeting developer workstations. They leverage trusted tools and stealthy techniques to harvest sensitive data and credentials.
goals: ["Harvest browser passwords, cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, messaging sessions, VPN profiles, Wi-Fi keys, and sensitive files", "Capture screenshots and system information", "Exfiltrate data to attacker-controlled FTP servers", "Compromise developer environments to access source code, cloud tokens, and production credentials"]
name: Evelyn Stealer Operators

description: Abused as an initial access vector by distributing a malicious extension through its marketplace, enabling attackers to execute their payload on developer machines.
name: Visual Studio Code

description: The legitimate screenshot tool is abused to load a malicious DLL (Lightshot.dll) as part of the attack chain, blending malicious activity with normal user actions.
name: Lightshot.exe

description: Used to launch hidden commands that download and execute additional payloads, facilitating further stages of the attack.
name: PowerShell

description: Abused as a protocol and service to exfiltrate stolen data and download additional malicious components from attacker-controlled servers.
name: FTP
